content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of size NNN.

In one operation, you can do the following:


Choose any two adjacent elements AiA_iAi​ and Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​ (1≤i<N)(1 \leq i \lt N)(1≤i<N).
Let X=gcd(Ai,Ai+1)X = gcd(A_i,A_{i+1})X=gcd(Ai​,Ai+1​) and Y=lcm(Ai,Ai+1)Y = lcm(A_i,A_{i+1})Y=lcm(Ai​,Ai+1​). Here gcdgcdgcd denotes the greatest common divisor and lcmlcmlcm denotes the least common multiple respectively.
Replace AiA_iAi​ with XXX and Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​ with YYY.
Find the **maximum** value of Σi=1NAi\Sigma^{N}_{i=1}A_{i}Σi=1N​Ai​ you can achieve, after performing the above operation any (possibly zero) number of times.

Since the result may be very large, you need to output the result modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of AAA.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output the **maximum** value of Σi=1NAi\Sigma^{N}_{i=1}A_{i}Σi=1N​Ai​ you can achieve, after performing the above operation any (possibly zero) number of times, modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer N – the size of the array. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible sum of the array after any number of allowed operations, modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Initially, A=[4,4,6,1]A=[4,4,6,1]A=[4,4,6,1].

Operation 111: Choose A2,A3A_2,A_3A2​,A3​. Thus, X=gcd(4,6)=2X = gcd(4, 6) = 2X=gcd(4,6)=2 and Y=lcm(4,6)=12Y = lcm(4, 6) = 12Y=lcm(4,6)=12. Thus AAA becomes [4,2,12,1][4,2,12,1][4,2,12,1].

The sum of the array is 4+2+12+1=194+2+12+1 = 194+2+12+1=19. It can be shown that this is the maximum sum of array we can achieve after applying any number of operations.
Test case 222: Initially, A=[108,24,18]A=[108,24,18]A=[108,24,18].

Operation 111: Choose A1,A2A_1,A_2A1​,A2​. Thus, X=gcd(108,24)=12X = gcd(108, 24) = 12X=gcd(108,24)=12 and Y=lcm(108,24)=216Y = lcm(108, 24) = 216Y=lcm(108,24)=216. Thus AAA becomes [12,216,18][12, 216, 18][12,216,18].
Operation 222: Choose A2,A3A_2,A_3A2​,A3​. Thus, X=gcd(216,18)=18X = gcd(216, 18) = 18X=gcd(216,18)=18 and Y=lcm(216,18)=216Y = lcm(216, 18) = 216Y=lcm(216,18)=216. Thus AAA becomes [12,18,216][12, 18, 216][12,18,216].
Operation 333: Choose A1,A2A_1,A_2A1​,A2​. Thus, X=gcd(12,18)=6X = gcd(12, 18) = 6X=gcd(12,18)=6 and Y=lcm(12,18)=36Y = lcm(12, 18) = 36Y=lcm(12,18)=36. Thus AAA becomes [6,36,216][6, 36, 216][6,36,216].

The sum of the array is 6+36+216=2586+36+216 = 2586+36+216=258. It can be shown that this is the maximum sum of array we can achieve after applying any number of operations.

solution.approach:
For a pair (a, b) the operation replaces it by (gcd(a,b), lcm(a,b)). The product a·b stays unchanged, while the sum never decreases – it strictly increases unless a divides b. Repeating the operation on all adjacent pairs sorts, for every prime p, the exponents of p in the array in non‑decreasing order. After the process finishes the array satisfies Ai | Ai+1 for all i and, for each prime, the multiset of its exponents is the same as initially but placed in sorted order. To maximise the total sum we have to align the largest exponents of different primes together (rearrangement inequality). Therefore the optimal final array is obtained by:
1. Factorising every Ai (Ai ≤ 10^6, so a sieve of smallest prime factors is enough).
2. For each prime p, collect the exponent of p in every position (zero if p does not divide Ai). Sort this list; zeros naturally appear first.
3. Build the answer array B of length N, initially all 1. For each prime p, multiply B[i] by p^{exp_i} where exp_i is the i‑th element of the sorted exponent list.
4. The required maximum sum is Σ B[i] (mod 1e9+7).
The algorithm runs in O(N log Amax + Σk log k) where k is the number of occurrences of a prime, which is ≤ total number of prime factors (≈ N·log Amax). Memory usage is O(N + total factors).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log Amax + totalFactors·log totalFactors) ≈ O(N log Amax)
